title: Tear Down the Barns
subtitle: Christ refuses to divide an inheritance, and warns against all greed

stillness: Be still, and do not carry your possessions in with you.
Speak within yourself, not aloud — as the rich man did, intra se dicens.
Let the heart that thinks only of gathering grow quiet.
gospel: One from the crowd said to him:
Master, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me.
But he said to him: Man, who has appointed me judge, or divider, over you?
And he said to them: Take heed, and beware of all covetousness;
for a man's life does not consist in the abundance of the things which he possesses.
And he spoke a parable to them, saying:
The field of a certain rich man brought forth plentiful fruits.
And he thought within himself, saying:
What shall I do, because I have no room to store my fruits?
And he said: This I will do —
I will pull down my barns, and will build greater;
and into them will I gather all that is grown to me, and my goods.
behold: See the man who comes out of the crowd, knowing Jesus to be just,
and yet asks him for a thing out of time and out of place:
to divide an earthly inheritance between brothers.
He had never tasted how good and how pleasant it is for brothers to dwell as one.
It was not law, but the love of having, that drew him —
he wished to circumvent his own brother.
And Christ, who came to gather and not to scatter, would not be his judge.
He did not come for the judgment of earthly possessions, but of heavenly ones.
Then behold the rich man whose field bore fruit beyond its custom,
more plentiful than the usual course of years.
And he was anxious — for abundance of riches brings care, and care brings anguish of soul.
He thinks within himself, lest he be overheard,
for the rich miser fears every man:
he sees a rich man and reckons him a robber; he sees a poor man and suspects a thief.
He was straitened by his own plenty —
wretched in his present goods, more wretched in those to come.
His field brought him not so much a harvest as heavier groans.
Care grew in him out of greed, and anguish out of his very fullness.
consider: He need not have enlarged his barns: good barns stood ready —
the bellies of the hungry poor.
But he did not remember the common nature of all,
nor could he think of anything but the gathering of earthly things,
not of the heavenly, whose storehouses are the poor of Christ.
And into these, he says, I will gather —
when on the contrary he ought to say: I will scatter, by giving to the poor.
All that is grown to me, he says, and my goods:
and in this he speaks a lie,
for it was out of iniquity that mine and thine were ever said,
since by the law of nature all things were common.
He counts temporal things his goods,
when a man's goods, insofar as he is a man, are spiritual, not earthly.
Gregory says: earthly possession is not man's, but heavenly.
Ambrose says: those are not a man's goods which he cannot carry away with him —
mercy alone goes with him.
Greed is a vice common to all: clerics and laymen, religious and worldly,
the small and the great — for all are bent on getting.
Beware of all covetousness, inward and outward,
the greedy seeking of temporal things and the tight holding of them.
For abundance does not lengthen your life:
more die of being full than of being thin.
And it is uncertain whether you will even reach the old age for whose sake you hoard.
prayer: Lord Jesus, you would not be a judge of inheritance or a divider of estates,
for you came to gather, not to scatter,
to make many to have one heart and one soul.
You are not the God of dissension, but of gathering, and of peace, and of unity.
Take from me, then, all covetousness, inward and outward,
the greedy seeking and the tight holding of passing things.
Teach me that my life does not consist in the abundance of what I possess,
and that earthly possession is not mine, but heavenly.
Let me not build greater barns,
but scatter, giving to the poor, who are your storehouses;
for of all I have, mercy alone will go with me.

latinJewel.latin: Non oportebat eum horrea dilatare, bene erant horrea parata, scilicet pauperum esurientium ventres.
latinJewel.english: He need not have enlarged his barns: good barns stood ready — the bellies of the hungry poor.
